headline: September 11, an Afghan war, and the century's first great financial reckonings
summary: 2001 was the year the post-Cold War decade ended. On 11 September al-Qaeda hijackers flew airliners into the World Trade Center and the Pentagon, killed nearly 3,000 people, and triggered the American-led invasion of Afghanistan on 7 October, ending Taliban rule by December. In November China joined the World Trade Organization at Doha, sealing a generation of globalisation. Argentina's economy collapsed in December, its president resigning amid rioting after the corralito froze bank accounts. The dot-com bust hollowed out Silicon Valley; Enron collapsed; the euro was in its last year as book-money before physical circulation in 2002. In Nepal a crown prince massacred his royal family. The world after 11 September felt suddenly and lastingly different.

snapshots.text: Europe in 2001 was preparing for the physical launch of the euro on 1 January 2002; twelve national central banks were coordinating the largest currency changeover in history under European Central Bank president Wim Duisenberg. Tony Blair's Labour won a second landslide in the UK general election on 7 June against William Hague's Conservatives; Gerhard Schroeder's SPD-Green coalition governed in Berlin; Jacques Chirac was preparing his 2002 re-election duel against Jean-Marie Le Pen; Silvio Berlusconi's centre-right coalition won the Italian election in May and returned him to power. In the Balkans the assassination of Serbian prime minister Zoran Djindjic still lay ahead, but Yugoslav president Vojislav Kostunica handed Slobodan Milosevic over to the ICTY at The Hague on 28 June for trial. In North Macedonia the Ohrid Framework Agreement of 13 August ended an ethnic Albanian insurgency. From 20-22 July the G8 met at Genoa; anti-globalisation protests culminated in the killing of Carlo Giuliani by a carabiniere. Foot-and-mouth disease devastated the British livestock sector. NATO agreed on the eve of 11 September to invoke Article 5 for the first time in its history in response to the American attacks.

The United States in 2001 was inaugurated under George W. Bush on 20 January after the disputed 2000 election. His first eight months focused on tax cuts, the Kyoto Protocol withdrawal and missile defence. On the morning of 11 September nineteen al-Qaeda hijackers, all but four Saudi nationals, took control of American Airlines Flight 11, United Airlines Flight 175, American 77 and United 93. AA 11 struck the North Tower of the World Trade Center at 8:46 am; UA 175 hit the South Tower at 9:03 am; both towers collapsed by 10:28 am. AA 77 struck the Pentagon at 9:37 am; UA 93 crashed in Shanksville, Pennsylvania at 10:03 am after passengers stormed the cockpit. 2,977 people were killed. Bush addressed Congress on 20 September and demanded the Taliban surrender Osama bin Laden. The Authorization for Use of Military Force was signed on 18 September; the USA PATRIOT Act on 26 October. In Argentina the government of Fernando de la Rua imposed the corralito bank freeze on 3 December; protests, looting and 39 deaths forced his resignation on 20-21 December, and the country defaulted on its debt. Brazil's Fernando Henrique Cardoso governed a stabilising economy. In Peru Alejandro Toledo succeeded Alberto Fujimori; in Mexico Vicente Fox governed his first year after ending 71 years of PRI rule.

China in 2001 formalised its integration into the global economy. On 10 November the WTO Ministerial Conference at Doha admitted the People's Republic as its 143rd member; accession took effect on 11 December, capping fifteen years of negotiation and locking in Deng Xiaoping's opening-and-reform trajectory under Jiang Zemin, Zhu Rongji and Hu Jintao. On 1 April a US EP-3 signals intelligence plane collided with a Chinese J-8 fighter over the South China Sea; the Chinese pilot Wang Wei died, the American crew was detained on Hainan for eleven days in the first foreign policy crisis of the Bush administration. On 13 July Beijing was awarded the 2008 Summer Olympics. In Japan Junichiro Koizumi became prime minister on 26 April promising radical reform of the LDP and postal privatisation; his August visit to Yasukuni Shrine provoked protests across East Asia. Japan's economy remained mired in deflation and the aftermath of the 1990s asset collapse. In South Korea Kim Dae-jung continued his Sunshine Policy toward North Korea; Kim Jong-il's regime in Pyongyang appeared briefly to reciprocate. Taiwan under Chen Shui-bian's DPP governed for the first year of the first non-KMT administration in island history.

South and Central Asia in 2001 lived through the shockwaves of 11 September. In Afghanistan the Taliban regime of Mullah Mohammed Omar, in power since 1996, had spent March dynamiting the Bamiyan Buddhas in defiance of world opinion; on 9 September Ahmad Shah Massoud of the Northern Alliance was assassinated by al-Qaeda operatives posing as journalists. On 7 October the United States and United Kingdom launched Operation Enduring Freedom, combining airstrikes with Northern Alliance ground operations and US special forces; Kabul fell on 13 November; Kandahar was surrendered by 7 December. The Bonn Conference of 5 December installed Hamid Karzai as chairman of an interim administration. In Pakistan General Pervez Musharraf, having taken power in the 1999 coup, made the pivotal decision to reverse Islamabad's Taliban support and become an American ally in the war on terror; on 20 June he formally assumed the presidency. In India the year saw the 26 January Gujarat earthquake that killed some 20,000 in Bhuj; the 13 December attack on the Indian Parliament by Lashkar-e-Taiba gunmen brought India and Pakistan to a border mobilisation crisis. In Nepal on 1 June Crown Prince Dipendra massacred King Birendra, Queen Aishwarya and eight other royals at Narayanhity Palace before shooting himself; King Gyanendra ascended amid a Maoist insurgency.

The Middle East and North Africa in 2001 were bracketed by the second Palestinian intifada, which had erupted in September 2000, and the reverberations of 11 September. On 17 October Israeli tourism minister Rehavam Ze'evi was assassinated in Jerusalem by the PFLP; Ariel Sharon's Likud-led national unity government, in power since March under a landslide against Ehud Barak, escalated raids into the West Bank. Yasser Arafat's Palestinian Authority besieged in Ramallah lost control over Hamas and Islamic Jihad suicide bombers. In Egypt Hosni Mubarak's regime detained thousands after 11 September on grounds of Islamist links; in Saudi Arabia the discovery that fifteen of the nineteen hijackers were Saudi nationals traumatised the House of Saud's relationship with the United States. In Turkey the November 2000 and February 2001 banking crises, capped by the currency's 40 percent devaluation on 22 February, forced Bulent Ecevit's government to accept IMF conditionality under finance minister Kemal Dervis. In Iran President Mohammad Khatami won re-election on 8 June with 78 percent of the vote in the last hopeful moment of the reformist era; his cooperation with Washington against the Taliban after 11 September went unrewarded by the January 2002 axis of evil speech. In Algeria the civil war of the 1990s ebbed; in Morocco King Mohammed VI pushed cautious reform.

Africa in 2001 saw a series of transitions and continuing crises. In July the Organisation of African Unity was formally dissolved at the Lusaka summit and replaced by the African Union, to be inaugurated at Durban in July 2002 under the presidency of Thabo Mbeki, who together with Olusegun Obasanjo of Nigeria and Abdoulaye Wade of Senegal launched the New Partnership for Africa's Development. In the Democratic Republic of the Congo Laurent-Desire Kabila was assassinated in his palace on 16 January; his son Joseph Kabila, at 29 the world's youngest head of state, took over and began the diplomacy that would end the Second Congo War (1998-2003) in which perhaps five million had died. In Sierra Leone a fragile peace under UNAMSIL began to disarm the RUF. In Angola the war between MPLA and UNITA continued but Jonas Savimbi's death in February 2002 lay just ahead. In Zimbabwe Robert Mugabe's fast-track land reform of 2000 was accelerating economic collapse. In South Africa the Treatment Action Campaign under Zackie Achmat led the litigation that would force Thabo Mbeki's AIDS-denialist government to permit anti-retroviral therapy; the Cape High Court ruled in December on nevirapine access. In Nigeria President Obasanjo grappled with sharia expansion in the north and Niger Delta militancy in the south.

Australia in 2001 celebrated the centenary of federation with a national year of commemoration marked by ceremonies in Melbourne on 9 May. Prime Minister John Howard's Liberal-National coalition, trailing Labor in the polls through the winter, was rescued politically by two events. On 26 August the Norwegian container ship MV Tampa rescued 433 asylum-seekers, mostly Hazaras fleeing Afghanistan, from a sinking Indonesian fishing boat and was refused entry to Christmas Island; Howard's Pacific Solution, sending the rescuees to Nauru and Manus Island, cemented a hard line on maritime asylum. Then came 11 September. Howard, in Washington on the day of the attacks, invoked ANZUS and committed Australian SAS and naval forces to Afghanistan; Labor's Kim Beazley acquiesced. Howard won the 10 November federal election. On 19 October the SIEV X sank in international waters between Indonesia and Christmas Island; 353 asylum-seekers drowned. In New Zealand Prime Minister Helen Clark's Labour-Alliance government sent SAS to Afghanistan. In Papua New Guinea the Bougainville Peace Agreement was signed on 30 August. In Fiji, in the wake of George Speight's 2000 coup, Laisenia Qarase's SDL government took power via the March election.

sources.title: The 9/11 Commission Report
sources.publisher: National Commission on Terrorist Attacks Upon the United States
